title: seanpatrickmay.github.io
oneLiner: this site. pulls my Garmin and Spotify data live, has writeups for all my projects, and it's just static HTML on GitHub Pages
cardDescription: my portfolio — live Garmin and Spotify widgets, a filterable project archive, and individual writeups for each project
proofPoints: 16 project pages all generated from one big JSON file
scheduled scripts pull fresh Garmin and Spotify data into static JSON
filterable project archive with search, tag filters, and shareable URL params
overview: this is a static-exported next.js portfolio that mixes hand-written project writeups with live data feeds from my Garmin and Spotify. the whole point is a fast personal site that still feels current without needing a backend running somewhere.
whatIBuilt: static next.js site with a homepage, project archive, and individual project pages
data refresh scripts that pull Garmin and Spotify summaries on a schedule
reusable UI components for cards, pills, filters, and the longer project writeups
howItWorks: scheduled scripts write JSON into public/
pages import that JSON at build time
github pages serves the whole thing as static HTML
results: fast static site that still feels like it has live activity widgets
shareable /projects/ and /projects/<slug>/ routes that work with static export
nextSteps: replace manual project metadata with something that syncs from github
add demo GIFs and before/after screenshots for the audit improvements
bullets: 16 project case-study pages with cover images, proof points, galleries, and deep-dive sections.
Live Garmin training stats (8-week rolling window, 60 recent activities) and Spotify top-10 via scheduled refresh scripts.
Project archive with full-text search, multi-tag/language filters, and sort by featured ranking, date, or title.

title: Life-Dashboard
oneLiner: my personal command center — it reads my iMessages, syncs my calendar, generates AI todos, tracks Garmin biometrics, and the whole thing is wrapped in a Monet-inspired pixel art UI
featuredDescription: personal command center: auto iMessage ingestion, calendar + project integration, AI-generated todos, and biometric tracking
cardDescription: auto iMessage ingestion, calendar sync, AI-generated todos, and Garmin biometrics in a pixel art Monet theme
proofPoints: auto imessage ingestion that parses conversations into action items, reminders, and context for the AI task generation
calendar + project integration that pulls events, deadlines, and repo activity into one daily view
openai responses api generates prioritized todos and daily briefings from messages, calendar, project state, and garmin data
overview: life-dashboard is basically a self-hosted personal OS. it auto-ingests my imessage conversations, syncs calendar events, tracks active projects, and generates AI-driven todos and daily briefings. the whole thing is rendered in this pixel art monet theme that shifts with time of day, and garmin biometrics give me a wellness baseline alongside the productivity stuff.
whatIBuilt: fastapi backend that handles imessage ingestion, calendar sync, project tracking, todo generation, and pulling garmin biometrics
react/vite frontend for the daily briefing, todo management, calendar view, project status, and chatting with the assistant
postgres data model for messages, calendar events, projects, todos, biometrics, and AI-generated insights
howItWorks: ingest imessage conversations and calendar events into postgres, pulling out actionable items and deadlines
aggregate messages, schedule, project activity, and biometrics into context for openai-powered todo and briefing generation
surface prioritized todos, daily briefings, and project status through one react dashboard
results: the whole loop from raw messages and calendar through AI prioritization to actionable daily output
clean separation between ingestion sources, AI reasoning, and the actual UI
nextSteps: add tighter evals for how relevant and well-prioritized the AI-generated todos actually are
pull in deeper git activity and PR status for the project integration
bullets: Auto iMessage ingestion parses conversations into structured action items, with calendar and project sync feeding a unified daily context window.
OpenAI Responses API generates prioritized todos and daily briefings from messages, schedule, project state, and Garmin biometrics.
Monet pixel art theme with 110+ sprites, four time-of-day palettes (morning/noon/twilight/night), and layered compositing.

title: LecteurAide
oneLiner: takes French PDFs and turns them into actual reading experiences — scene segmentation, sentence-by-sentence translation, vocab extraction, and quizzes. i built it because looking things up word by word was killing me
featuredDescription: French reading companion — Gemini splits books into scenes with translation, vocab, and comprehension quizzes.
cardDescription: French reading companion — Gemini splits books into scenes with translation, vocab, and comprehension quizzes
proofPoints: six-stage pipeline: PDF extraction, heading stripping, sentence segmentation, cloud translation, chunking, and gemini annotation
context-aware scene segmentation that passes previous scene summaries forward so the narrative stays coherent across chunks
SSE-streaming upload progress, keyboard-navigable scene reader, and click-to-jump vocab highlighting
overview: lecteuraide is a french reading companion for long PDFs. you give it a book, it segments it into scenes, translates every sentence, extracts vocabulary, and generates comprehension questions, then presents everything in a purpose-built reading interface. i built it because looking up words one at a time was really painful.
whatIBuilt: fastapi backend for book upload, scene segmentation, translation, vocab extraction, and persistence
next.js frontend for uploading, browsing the library, and reading scene by scene
vertex AI and google cloud translation pipeline that turns raw PDFs into guided reading experiences
howItWorks: upload a french PDF and title through the ingestion API
segment the text into scenes and annotate with translations, vocab, and questions
persist the processed book and render it through the web reader
results: turns static PDFs into an interactive reading workflow
the whole thing from document ingestion through translation to reader UX in one product
nextSteps: record a walkthrough video of the scene-by-scene reading flow
build out stronger review and spaced-repetition loops in the reader
bullets: Six-stage FastAPI pipeline: PDF text extraction, sentence segmentation, batch Cloud Translation (150 sentences per call), and Gemini-driven scene annotation.
Context-aware scene segmentation with deduplication, vocabulary grouped by part of speech, and 4 comprehension questions per scene.
Next.js reader with SSE upload streaming, keyboard scene navigation, adjustable text scale, and click-to-sentence vocabulary lookup.

title: Human Digit Recognition
oneLiner: two PyTorch CNNs — first one finds your hand in the frame, second one counts how many fingers you're holding up. trained on a dataset i annotated myself
featuredDescription: two-stage CV pipeline: find the hand, then count the fingers. custom dataset and a Flask demo
cardDescription: two-stage CV pipeline: find the hand, then count the fingers. custom dataset and a Flask demo
proofPoints: two CNNs (~7.5M params each): 4-layer conv stack + 9-layer dense head on 28x28 grayscale inputs
custom dataset with a canvas-based bounding-box annotation tool and albumentations augmentation
cross-platform inference with automatic CUDA/MPS/CPU device detection
overview: this is a two-stage perception project, first it detects a hand region in an image, then classifies how many fingers are raised. i built the whole thing from the annotation tooling through training to a flask demo you can actually interact with.
whatIBuilt: pytorch models for hand-box detection and digit-count classification
annotation tooling and notebooks for training and evaluation
flask inference server and browser-facing prediction flow
howItWorks: capture or upload an image
predict a hand bounding box from the full frame
crop the detected region and run digit classification
return the predicted count through the flask API
results: the whole pipeline from labeling images to actually serving predictions
shows staged CV inference rather than just a single-model toy demo
nextSteps: clean up dependencies for easier reproducible setup
capture an inference demo GIF showing hand detection through digit classification
bullets: Stage 1 CNN predicts hand bounding box; stage 2 crops and classifies finger count (0-9) via argmax over 10 logits.
Custom annotation tool (Flask + canvas) and albumentations augmentation pipeline with no external dataset dependency.
End-to-end inference auto-detects CUDA, Apple MPS, or CPU and handles PIL, numpy, or file path inputs.

title: Wildfire Modeling
oneLiner: research pipeline that pulls GOES satellite data through Earth Engine, converts it to GOFER-format JSON, and runs regressions on how fires spread. Python, JavaScript, and R all in one repo
cardDescription: wildfire research — satellite data downloads, JSON normalization, logistic regressions, and a canvas viewer for the outputs
proofPoints: earth engine download and normalization pipeline
locational spread + continuation regression workflows
lightweight local viewer for GOFER-style JSON outputs
overview: this is a research workflow for collecting wildfire geospatial data, converting it into GOFER-style JSON, and running local regression analyses on fire spread and continuation. it basically sits between raw remote-sensing data and whatever modeling experiments i want to run downstream.
whatIBuilt: CLI scripts for downloading GOES confidence stacks and aligned RTMA meteorology
conversion tools that normalize geospatial outputs into modeling-friendly JSON
regression workflows and notebooks for single-fire and multi-fire analysis
howItWorks: download wildfire confidence stacks and aligned meteorology
convert outputs into GOFER-style JSON time series
run locational regressions and inspect outputs through notebooks or the local viewer
results: repeatable research workflow from earth engine exports to regression outputs
supports both single-fire analysis and broader multi-fire experiments
nextSteps: tighten environment bootstrapping and add clearer example outputs
expand the multiresolution data path and result visualization
bullets: 15 Python CLI scripts, 18 Earth Engine JavaScript modules, and 6 R post-processing scripts across the full pipeline.
Downloads GOES confidence stacks and hourly RTMA meteorology (temp, wind, humidity) with adaptive multi-band chunking.
Canvas-based web viewer with 5-stop color gradient, time-slider navigation, and metadata display for GOFER JSON outputs.

title: Sentiment Market MM
oneLiner: automated market maker for prediction markets — ingests sentiment signals, prices two-sided quotes, manages inventory risk, and executes across Kalshi and Polymarket
cardDescription: prediction market MM with sentiment-driven spread pricing, inventory risk management, and a React dashboard for monitoring everything
proofPoints: sentiment-weighted spread pricing that adjusts bid-ask width based on news flow, social volume, and order book imbalance
inventory risk engine with position limits, greeks-inspired exposure tracking, and dynamic skew to reduce directional risk
multi-venue execution layer with order lifecycle management across Kalshi and Polymarket
overview: this is a full-stack market-making system for prediction markets. it ingests real-time sentiment signals, prices two-sided quotes with dynamic spread adjustment, manages inventory exposure, and executes across multiple venues through a unified order management layer.
whatIBuilt: fastapi backend with sentiment ingestion, spread pricing engine, order management, and position tracking
postgres + redis schema for markets, quotes, orders, fills, positions, and real-time sentiment state
react monitoring dashboard for live P&L, position exposure, spread visualization, and order flow
howItWorks: ingest sentiment signals from news APIs, social feeds, and order book data into a scoring pipeline
price two-sided quotes with spread width driven by sentiment confidence, volatility, and current inventory skew
execute and manage order lifecycle across venues with fill tracking and position reconciliation
results: the whole market-making loop from signal ingestion through quote pricing to execution and risk tracking
gives me a sandbox for testing different spread strategies against real prediction market data
nextSteps: add a backtesting harness to evaluate spread strategies against historical order book snapshots
implement adaptive position sizing based on realized P&L and drawdown thresholds
bullets: Sentiment scoring pipeline aggregates news, social, and order-flow signals into a real-time confidence score that drives dynamic bid-ask spread width.
Inventory risk manager tracks net exposure per market with position limits, directional skew adjustment, and Greeks-style sensitivity metrics.
Unified execution layer abstracts Kalshi and Polymarket APIs behind a common order interface with fill tracking and position reconciliation.

title: PokerML
oneLiner: CFR+ solver for heads-up no limit — k-means card abstraction, 100K iterations of external sampling, and a web UI where you can play the bot and see its ranges live
cardDescription: poker solver with 100K iterations of CFR+, card bucketing, and a web app with live 13x13 range heatmaps
proofPoints: CFR+ with external sampling: 100K iterations, regret clamping, bucket-cached traversal across all 4 streets
k-means preflop abstraction (10 buckets) + empirical CDF postflop bucketing with sampled board completions
fastapi web UI where you play against the bot with a live 13x13 range heatmap showing action probabilities per hand
overview: this is a heads-up no-limit hold'em solver built on CFR+ and external sampling monte carlo. it trains over 100K iterations with k-means preflop bucketing and percentile-based postflop abstraction, then serves the converged strategy through a fastapi web app where you can play against the bot and see its 13x13 range grid in real time.
whatIBuilt: CFR+ trainer with external sampling MCCFR, immutable game states, and per-iteration bucket caching for speed
two-level card abstraction: k-means on preflop equity (169 hands to 10 buckets) and percentile CDF on postflop hand strength
action abstraction with 50% and 100% pot bet sizings, fold/check/call, all-in, and a 4-raise-per-street cap
fastapi server with session management, bot inference, and a 13x13 range grid API for the frontend
howItWorks: deal hands and board, pre-cache card buckets for both players across all 4 streets
traverse the game tree: enumerate all actions for the traversing player, sample one for the opponent
update cumulative regrets (clamped to zero for CFR+) and accumulate strategy sums
after 100K iterations, compress the averaged strategy to JSON and serve it via fastapi for live play
results: converged strategy across ~1,173 lines of python with an 18.5 MB compressed strategy file
interactive web UI where you can play full hands and inspect the bot's action distribution for any of 169 canonical hands
nextSteps: add variance reduction techniques like pruning and warm starting
expand action abstraction with more bet sizings and raise depths

coverImage.alt: PokerML preflop range matrix and decision tree

bullets: CFR+ with external sampling: 100K iterations, regret clamping, and per-deal bucket caching across preflop through river.
K-means preflop bucketing (169 hands to 10 clusters) + empirical CDF postflop abstraction with sampled completions.
FastAPI web app with live 13x13 range heatmap: color-coded actions, per-hand drill-down, and bot play sessions.

title: MBTA Leave Now
oneLiner: tells you when to leave for the T — matches trips across lines, adds walking and transfer time, and gives you a leave-by recommendation
cardDescription: MBTA commute solver — multi-leg trip matching with walk and transfer buffers, plus a leave-by planner
proofPoints: MBTA prediction-driven leave-now API
fixed commute leave-by planner with transfer and walking buffers
spring boot + gradle kotlin DSL practice project
overview: this is a spring boot project that answers a pretty simple commuter question: when should i leave? it has both a generic stop-level API and a more opinionated leave-by planner for my fixed commute. i mostly built it to get comfortable with spring and gradle.
whatIBuilt: spring MVC endpoints for stop-level leave guidance and a fixed commute planner
MBTA API client using java HttpClient and jackson parsing
minimal browser UI for entering a deadline and viewing leave-by guidance
howItWorks: fetch upcoming MBTA predictions and schedules for the relevant stops
apply walk-time and transfer buffers to compute a leave-by recommendation
expose results through a REST API and a lightweight static frontend
results: solid practice project for gradle kotlin DSL, spring MVC, and test structure
bridges backend API logic and a usable frontend in one small repo
nextSteps: generalize the fixed commute planner into configurable routes
add example responses and screenshots for easier onboarding
bullets: Fetches real-time MBTA predictions and schedules, filtering by stop, route, and direction with paginated responses.
Multi-leg commute solver matches trip IDs across Orange and Red lines with configurable walk-time and transfer buffers.
Headway bunching detection via coefficient-of-variation scoring to assess service reliability.

title: Study Buddy
oneLiner: give it your PDFs and lecture notes, it gives you back Anki flashcard decks and LaTeX cheat sheets weighted by difficulty. built with CrewAI agents and ChromaDB for retrieval
cardDescription: upload your notes, get back Anki decks and difficulty-weighted cheat sheets. CrewAI agents and ChromaDB under the hood
proofPoints: document ingestion -> flashcards -> cheat-sheet workflow
RAG-backed study flow using ChromaDB
fastapi app with a focused single-page interface
overview: study-buddy is a study companion that takes your lecture notes, research papers, and anki exports and turns them into curated flashcards, decks, and cheat sheets. it combines LLM tooling, retrieval, and a simple web interface into one study workflow.
whatIBuilt: built the full ingestion-to-artifact pipeline for processing study materials and generating outputs
fastapi-backed flow for converting source material into flashcards and cheat sheets
RAG path using chromaDB to keep generations anchored in the uploaded material
howItWorks: upload notes, papers, or deck exports
index material into a retrieval layer
generate flashcards, exports, and cheat-sheet style outputs from the grounded context
results: working prototype that shows a concrete AI-assisted study loop from raw notes to review artifacts
nextSteps: show sample flashcard deck and cheat sheet outputs in the readme
add a one-click demo path with a bundled example PDF

coverImage.alt: Study Buddy flashcard and cheat sheet generator

bullets: CrewAI agents (flashcard, cheatsheet, RAG, aggregator) orchestrate a multi-step pipeline from document ingestion to artifact export.
ChromaDB vector store with LangChain embeddings grounds all generation in uploaded source material via similarity search.
Exports Anki .apkg decks and compiles difficulty-weighted LaTeX cheat sheets to PDF, using Anki review history for prioritization.

title: N-Queens Genetic Algorithm
oneLiner: genetic algorithm for N-Queens — tournament selection, adaptive mutation rate, solves N=8 in under 500 generations
cardDescription: GA solver with tournament selection, single-point crossover, and adaptive mutation. solves N=8 in around 500 generations
proofPoints: tournament selection + single-point crossover
mutation schedule that scales with board size
CLI/.env configurable experiments
overview: this project explores the n-queens puzzle through a compact genetic algorithm. it's intentionally small and transparent, so it's easy to see how population size, mutation behavior, and iteration caps affect convergence.
whatIBuilt: single-file genetic algorithm with tournament selection and crossover
collision-based fitness function for scoring candidate boards
CLI and .env configuration for quick experimentation
howItWorks: initialize a population of candidate boards
score conflicts and select parents via tournaments
apply crossover and mutation across generations
stop when a zero-collision solution is found or the iteration cap is hit
results: nice compact reference implementation for an evolutionary search workflow
nextSteps: add seeded benchmarking and clearer example outputs
try alternate encodings or hybrid local-search ideas

coverImage.alt: N-Queens genetic algorithm solution with convergence chart

bullets: Tournament selection (k=3) picks two parents; single-point crossover produces offspring; 1/N per-gene mutation adapts to board size.
Population of 200, cap of 200K generations, all configurable via .env or CLI—solves N=8 in under 500 generations.
Collision-counting fitness function runs O(N^2) pairwise checks across rows, columns, and both diagonals.

title: Maze-Game
oneLiner: generates mazes with Kruskal's algorithm, then you can watch BFS or DFS solve them frame by frame, or just play through it yourself with arrow keys
cardDescription: maze generator with animated BFS and DFS solvers, plus a manual play mode with arrow keys
proofPoints: maze generation via weighted graph + spanning tree
BFS and DFS solver visualization
interactive player mode with keyboard controls
overview: maze-game is a java world program that generates solvable mazes, animates breadth-first or depth-first search, and has a manual play mode. it's sort of a compact mix of graph generation, search visualization, and event-driven interaction.
whatIBuilt: maze generator over a grid graph using weighted edges and union-find style logic
solver playback for BFS and DFS so you can actually watch pathfinding instead of just getting the answer
manual mode with movement, collision checks, and win detection
howItWorks: generate a maze from weighted connections across a grid
choose BFS or DFS to visualize a solver path
optionally switch into manual mode and play through the maze yourself
results: solid early project for graph algorithms, search, and interactive java state
nextSteps: record a GIF of maze generation and BFS/DFS solver animation
document the course-library setup more precisely

coverImage.alt: Maze generation and BFS solution path

bullets: Kruskal's algorithm with union-find on a weighted grid graph; supports horizontal/vertical edge bias for varied maze shapes.
Tick-based animation renders BFS or DFS search one node per frame (0.01s), then traces the solution path in green.
Manual mode with arrow-key movement, collision validation against maze connections, and a win-state overlay at the exit.

title: Hex Reversi
oneLiner: Reversi on a hex grid — 48-class MVC architecture with cube coordinates, composable AI strategies, and a full adapter layer for plugging in third-party code
cardDescription: hex Reversi with composable AI strategies, cube-coordinate geometry, and adapter-pattern integration for third-party providers
proofPoints: cube-coordinate hex board (q+r+s=0) with odd-r offset conversion and configurable side length
TryTwo combinator chains strategies (corners -> avoid-near-corners -> minimax -> capture-max) with fallible/infallible pattern
four adapter classes bridge third-party provider code without modifying core
overview: hex reversi is an extensible reversi/othello implementation on a hexagonal grid. the main emphasis is software design, clean MVC separation, adapters for different play modes, and modular AI strategies you can mix and match.
whatIBuilt: hex-grid reversi engine with configurable board shapes and starting states
MVC architecture with GUI + text views and a controller layer
strategy system including minimax and composable heuristics
howItWorks: represent the board on an odd-r hex coordinate system
validate moves and apply flips according to reversi rules
run players (human/AI) through a controller loop
plug different strategies into the AI player
nextSteps: add stronger evaluation heuristics and time controls
improve UI polish and strategy visualization
bullets: 48-class MVC architecture with cube-coordinate hex board, observer-driven controller, and Swing GUI with hexagon rendering and move previewing.
TryTwo combinator composes four strategies (corner capture, corner avoidance, 2-ply minimax, max capture) with fallible/infallible type safety.
Adapter layer (4 classes, ~600 LOC) integrates third-party provider code without touching core implementation—Strategy, Observer, Factory, and Decorator patterns throughout.

title: Linux Shell (C)
oneLiner: custom shell in 793 lines of C — recursive piping at arbitrary depth, I/O redirection, and a hand-rolled dynamic vector for tokenization
cardDescription: 793-line C shell with recursive piping, I/O redirection, quote-aware tokenizer, and 5 built-in commands
proofPoints: recursive pipe chaining supports arbitrary-depth pipelines via right-to-left parsing and fork/dup2
5 built-ins (cd, source, prev, help, verbose) communicate with parent via a dedicated global IPC pipe
single-pass tokenizer handles quoted strings, special characters, and whitespace with a custom 2x-growth dynamic vector
overview: a custom shell written in C with command execution, piping, redirection, and signal handling. the focus is on process control and getting correctness right under typical shell behaviors.
whatIBuilt: command execution with piping and I/O redirection
signal handling and process orchestration
built-in commands (source, prev, cd, help, verbose toggle)
howItWorks: parse input into commands and pipeline segments
fork/exec child processes and wire pipes between them
apply redirections and handle signals
track history for built-ins like prev
nextSteps: add job control (fg/bg) if i feel like it
harden parsing for edge cases and quoting rules
bullets: Recursive pipe architecture: right-to-left parsing paired with recursive fork/dup2 elegantly chains arbitrary-depth pipelines.
6-layer execution stack (command lines → pipes → redirects → fork/exec) with global IPC pipe for built-in cd/source communication.
Custom dynamic vector (2x growth, string-copying) backs a single-pass tokenizer that handles quotes, special chars, and delimiters.
